elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the rigidity of the Boolean algebra $P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$, for $\lambda$ inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-002: For $\lambda=\omega$ there is extensive literature on this topic (see, e.g., the survey~\cite{farah2024corona ); some general results on $P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\kappa}$ can be found in~\cite{MR3480121 .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: In~\cite{Sh:1224 it was shown, for $\lambda$ inaccessible and $2^\lambda=\lambda^{++}$, that conistently every automorphism is densely trivial.}
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper we show: \begin{equation}\tag{Thm.~\ref{thm:a}}0.8\textwidth{\thetext}\end{equation} Note that the weaker variant ``there is a nontrivial automorphism'' follows from~\cite[Lem.~3.2]{Sh:990 (the proof there was faulty, and fixed in~\cite{Sh:990a ); and for $\lambda$ measurable, a proof (again only for ``nontrivial'') was given in~\cite{Sh:1224 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: We also show: \begin{equation}\tag{Thm.~\ref{thm:b}}0.8\textwidth{It is consistent that $\lambda$ is inaccessible, $2^\lambda$ an arbitrary regular cardinal, and that there is a nowhere trivial automorphism of $\mathcal P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$.}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\tag{Thm.~\ref{thm:b}}0.8\textwidth{It is consistent that $\lambda$ is inaccessible, $2^\lambda$ an arbitrary regular cardinal, and that there is a nowhere trivial automorphism of $\mathcal P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-006: We will always assume that $\lambda$ is inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-007: For $A\subseteq \lambda$, $[A]^{<\lambda}$ denotes the subsets of $A$ of size less than $\lambda$; and $[A]^{\lambda}$ those of size $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-008: With $[A]$ we denote the equivalence class of $A$ modulo $[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$.
elements.sent-2411-009: We write $A=^*B$ for $[A]=[B]$, and $A\subseteq^* B$ for $|A\setminus B|<\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, we also use $f[A]:=\{f(a):\, a\in A\}$.
elements.sent-2411-011: So for example $[f[A]]$ is the equivalence class of the $f$-image of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-012: We consider $\mathcal P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$ as Boolean algebra.
elements.sent-2411-013: A (Boolean algebra) automorphism $\pi$ of $\mathcal P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$ is called trivial on $A$ (for $A\in [\lambda]^\lambda$) if there is an $f\in\Sym(\lambda)$ such that $\pi([B])=[f[B]]$ for all $B\subseteq A$.
elements.sent-2411-014: For $\delta\le\lambda$, $C\subseteq \delta$ closed and nonempty, and $\alpha\in C$, we set \begin{equation}I^*(C\subseteq \delta, \alpha):=\big\{\beta:\, \alpha\le \beta<\min\big((C\cup\{\delta\})\setminus (\alpha+1)\big)\big\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-015: So the $I^*(C\subseteq \delta, \alpha)$, for $\alpha\in C$, form an increasing interval partition of $\delta\setminus\min(C)$.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this section, we define the set $\AP$ of ``approximations''.
elements.sent-2411-017: An approximation $\bfa$ will induce a ``partial monomorphism'' $\btpi^\bfa$ defined on some $\bftB^\bfa$ which is trivial, i.e., generated by some $\bfpi^\bfa\in\Sym(\lambda)$.
elements.sent-2411-018: We will use such approximations to build a nowhere trivial automorphism $\btphi$ as limit (i.e., $\btphi\restriction \bftB^ \bfa=\btpi^ \bfa$), cf.~Fact~\ref{fact:central .}
elements.sent-2411-020: So the $I^\bfa_\veps$ form an increasing interval partition of $\lambda\setminus\min(\bf\bfC^\bfa)$; and $\bfpi^\bfa\restriction I^\bfa_\veps\in\Sym(I^\bfa_\veps)$.
elements.sent-2411-022: If $(\bfa_i)_{i<\delta}$ is an $\le_\AP $ increasing chain such that $\bigcup_{i<\delta \bftB^{\bfa_i =P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$, then $\btphi:=\bigcup_{i<\delta \btpi^{\bfa_i}$ is an Boolean algebra monomorphism of $P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: If additionally $\bigcup_{i<\delta \btpi^{\bfa_i [\bftB^{\bfa_i ]=P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$, then $\btphi$ is an automorphism.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: We use $\vee$ and $^c$ for the Boolean-algebra-operations, i.e., $[A\cup B]=[A]\vee[B]$, and $[A]^c=[\lambda\setminus A]$.
elements.sent-2411-025: It is enough to show that $\btphi$ is injective, honors $\vee$ and $^c$, and maps $[\emptyset]$ to itself.
elements.sent-2411-026: For $X_1,X_2$ in $P(\lambda)/[\lambda]^{<\lambda}$ there is an $i<\delta$ and some $A_1,A_2,A_\text{union}$ in $\bfB^{\bfa_i}$, such that $[A_j]=X_j$ for $j=1,2$ and $[A_\text{union ]=[A_1\cup A_2]=X_1\vee X_2$.}
